examples.title: Dynamic 1D Array Creation and Usage
examples.description: Demonstrates creating, using, and freeing a dynamic 1D integer array in C
examples.explanation: This program demonstrates the complete lifecycle of dynamic memory allocation: requesting memory with malloc, checking for allocation failure, using the array, and freeing the memory. Note the use of (int*) cast and sizeof(int) for portability.

examples.title: Dynamic 2D Array Using Pointer-to-Pointer
examples.description: Creates a 2D array where each row is separately allocated
examples.explanation: This creates a true 2D array using pointer-to-pointer technique. Each row is a separate allocation, allowing for ragged arrays (variable row lengths). Memory must be freed in reverse order of allocation to avoid leaks.
examples.timeComplexity: O(rows * cols) for initialization and display
examples.spaceComplexity: O(rows * cols) for the 2D array plus O(rows) for row pointers

examples.title: Dynamic List as Dynamic Array in Python
examples.description: Demonstrates how Python lists work as dynamic arrays with automatic resizing
examples.code:
# Python's list is a dynamic array
# No explicit malloc/free needed - automatic memory management

# Create a dynamic list
dynamic_list = []  # Starts empty

# Append elements - list grows automatically
for i in range(10):
    dynamic_list.append(i * 10)
    print(f"Added {i*10}, Current length: {len(dynamic_list)}")

# Access elements like regular array
print(f"Element at index 5: {dynamic_list[5]}")

# Python handles:
# - Initial allocation
# - Automatic resizing (typically doubles when full)
# - Memory deallocation when list goes out of scope
# - No explicit memory management needed

# Demonstrate growth behavior
import sys
sizes = []
for i in range(20):
    dynamic_list.append(i)
    # Get memory size of list object
    sizes.append(sys.getsizeof(dynamic_list))
    
print(f"\nCapacity growth: {sizes}")
print("Notice: capacity increases in chunks, not one-by-one")
examples.explanation: Python lists demonstrate dynamic array concepts internally: they allocate a buffer larger than needed, and when full, allocate a larger buffer (typically double the size) and copy elements. This is why append is usually O(1) amortized. The growth pattern shows memory being allocated in chunks.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) amortized per append, O(n) for inserting at beginning
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is number of elements

examples.title: Dynamic Array with Auto-Growth Implementation
examples.description: Implements a dynamic array similar to C++ vectors with automatic capacity doubling
examples.explanation: This implementation shows how dynamic arrays grow: start with initial capacity (2), when full, double the capacity using realloc(). This gives O(1) amortized time for push operations. The key insight is that occasional expensive reallocations are offset by many cheap appends. Always check realloc() return value before updating the pointer.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) amortized per pushBack, O(n) for full resize
